name: Mammata Bhatta
dates_notes: Mammaṭa is generally placed in late-eleventh- to early-twelfth-century Kashmir; the precise dates remain approximate.
core_thesis: Mammaṭa Bhaṭṭa is the author of the *Kāvyaprakāśa*, a compact and highly influential synthesis of Sanskrit poetics. His work is not a metaphysical system; it is a technical account of what counts as *kāvya*, how word and meaning contribute to poetic force, how defects, qualities, figures, and suggestion are to be distinguished, and how *rasa* functions within poetic experience. The treatise absorbs the post-Ānandavardhana settlement that *dhvani* and suggested meaning must be included in poetics, while arranging the topic as a teachable śāstra. The on-disk text includes the well-known definition of poetry as faultless word and meaning with qualities, sometimes ornamented, and with excellence where suggested meaning predominates ([*Kāvyaprakāśa* kārikā 4](cite://mammata/kavyaprakasa/karika-4)). Later Gauḍīya authors cite Mammaṭa as the classical aesthetic baseline when arguing for *bhakti-rasa*.

engaged_works.title: Kavyaprakasa
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Conventionally assigned to Mammaṭa's late-eleventh- or early-twelfth-century Kashmiri milieu.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely ascribed to Mammaṭa in the Sanskrit poetics tradition and in modern scholarship.

ascription_evidence.description: Poetics commentarial and scholastic tradition transmits the work as Mammaṭa's.

engaged_works.summary: A systematic Sanskrit poetics manual that treats the aims of poetry, poetic cause, definition of *kāvya*, verbal powers, defects, qualities, figures, suggestion, and rasa. The text became a standard baseline for later discussions because it combines the earlier alaṅkāra, dhvani, and rasa materials into a compact curricular form. For this project it is especially useful where Vedānta-adjacent bhakti authors appeal to classical poetics to argue that devotional emotion can be treated as a genuine *rasa*.
